frameworks.name: evidence accumulation
frameworks.one_line: integration of noisy sensory pulses through time to drive perceptual decisions

frameworks.name: feedback-based multiplicative gain control
frameworks.one_line: accumulated evidence multiplicatively modulates sensory response amplitudes

frameworks.name: Weber-Fechner Law
frameworks.one_line: classical proportional discrimination threshold contrasted with observed deviations

frameworks.name: multiplicative feedback-loop circuit model
frameworks.one_line: circuit synthesizing accumulation and gain control to explain perceptual deviations

core_question: How do brains turn noisy, sparse sensory pulses into accumulated decisions, and what circuit mechanism reconciles accumulation with selective gain amplification?
key_assumptions: sensory responses can be time-locked to discrete evidence pulses
accumulation and gain control are not mutually exclusive mechanisms
amplitude modulations reflect computationally meaningful signals
manipulations: randomly timed pulses of visual evidence
key_findings: putative sensory neurons time-locked their responses to each evidence pulse
sensory response amplitudes were modulated by choice and accumulated evidence
modulations resemble gain-like effects across the population
interpretations: a multiplicative feedback architecture parsimoniously explains deviations from Weber-Fechner Law
accumulation and feedback gain hypotheses can be synthesized within one circuit
